tossups.question: In an early piece, this composer used the Phrygian mode for an antiphon to the Virgin Mary, Salve intemerata. This composer included “Magnificat” and “Nunc dimittis” sections in his Short Service in the Dorian Mode. A contemporary ensemble named for this composer’s “Scholars” helped to repopularize early a capella vocal music. A work by this composer that ends “respice humilitatem nostram” is scored for eight (*) choirs of five voices and was written for a monarch’s fortieth birthday. A later composer’s orchestral work reuses a Phrygian melody that this composer wrote in honor of Archbishop Parker, “Why fum’th in fight.” This composer and his student, William Byrd, were given a monopoly on publishing polyphonic music by Elizabeth I. For 10 points, name this English Renaissance composer who wrote Spem in alium and inspired a Fantasia by Ralph (rafe) Vaughan (von) Williams.
tossups.question_sanitized: In an early piece, this composer used the Phrygian mode for an antiphon to the Virgin Mary, Salve intemerata. A later composer's orchestral work reuses a Phrygian melody that this composer wrote in honor of Archbishop Parker, "Why fum'th in fight." This composer and his student, William Byrd, were given a monopoly on publishing polyphonic music by Elizabeth I. For 10 points, name this English Renaissance composer who wrote Spem in alium and inspired a Fantasia by Ralph (rafe) Vaughan (von) Williams.

tossups.question: A composer with this first name wrote a treatise in which Master Gnorimus teaches music to brothers Poly·mathes and Philo·manthes. An organist with this first name compiled a book of 121 keyboard pieces, most of which are based on Catholic liturgical music, called The Mulliner Book. A composer with this first name wrote the most popular setting of “In ieiunio et fletu” (“yay-YOO-nee-oh et FLEH-too”) and included pieces nicknamed his canon, his ordinal, and the “third (*) mode melody” in a psalter written for Archbishop Matthew Parker. That composer with this first name and his student were granted a 21-year monopoly on polyphonic music by Elizabeth I (“the first”). For 10 points, give the first name of that composer who taught William Byrd, wrote the 40-voice motet Spem in alium (“AH-lee-um”), and had the last name Tallis.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Thomas [accept Thomas Morley; accept Thomas Mulliner; accept Thomas Tallis]

For 10 points, give the first name of that composer who taught William Byrd, wrote the 40-voice motet Spem in alium ("AH-lee-um"), and had the last name Tallis.

tossups.question: Agitation from supporters of this cause led to the production of the “Educational Series” of certificates. A faction of Republicans named for their support of this cause was led by Colorado senator Henry M. Teller. The only overridden veto of Rutherford B. Hayes was a bill supporting this cause. In support of this cause, the Bland–Allison Act reversed a law that supporters of this cause called the “Crime of ‘73.” Supporters of this cause advocated for a “16-to-1” weight ratio. A speech in support of this cause stated that “You shall not press down upon the brow of labor this crown of thorns.” For 10 points, Populists such as William Jennings Bryan supported what cause that advocated using multiple precious metals as currency?
tossups.answer: bimetallism [or free silver; accept Silverites; accept the Silver Republican Party; accept descriptions of using silver as currency or using gold and silver as currency; prompt on using silver or using gold]

tossups.question: Signum Records began as an effort to record this composer’s work, which includes a namesake “canon” published by Thomas Ravenscroft. Alessandro Striggio’s Ecce Beatam Lucem possibly inspired the number of performers for a piece by this composer in which rotating points of imitation end with “polyphonic detailism.” Matthew Parker’s version of Psalm No. 2 serves as the text for a (*) “Third Mode Melody” by this composer that forms the basis of a much later piece for double string orchestra and string quartet. The elegy Ye Sacred Muses was written to commemorate this composer by his student William Byrd. This composer’s song “Why fum’th in fight” was adapted into a Fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams. For 10 points, name this Renaissance-era English composer of the 40-person motet Spem in alium.
tossups.answer: Thomas Tallis [accept Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Thomas Tallis [accept Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis]

tossups.question: This composer used motifs from his Phrygian (“FRIJ-ee-in”) antiphon Salve intemerata for a five-part mass of the same name. This composer used a vernacular text from the Gospel of John for his four-part motet “If ye love me.” Peter Phillips founded an a cappella group specializing in sacred music called this musician’s “Scholars.” This composer likely intended for eight five-voice choirs to stand in a (*) horseshoe around the audience in a work that sets to music a prayer adapted from the Book of Judith. A 1575 monopoly on polyphonic music was granted to this man and his student, William Byrd. This man’s psalter tune “Why Fum’th in Fight” was the thematic basis of a much later work for string orchestra by the composer of A Lark Ascending. For 10 points, name this Renaissance composer of Spem in alium who inspired a fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

tossups.question: Barry Eichengreen analyzed the U.S.’s lack of “cooperative standards” supporting this policy internationally during the 1920s and 30s in a book whose title likens it to “fetters.” This policy was restored by a compromise bill that addressed the issue of “shin plasters” and was engineered by George Edmunds. Historians generally regard the 1870s through 1914 as the “classical” period of this policy, whose end was challenged in an article asking “What Has the Government Done?” by Murray Rothbard. The last vestige of this policy in the U.S. was ended with a 1971 directive to John Connally, which prompted the “Nixon shock” and disrupted Bretton-Woods. A speech named for opposing this policy warned to not “press down upon the brow of labor this crown of thorns.” For 10 points, name this policy that William Jennings Bryan said “mankind” should not be “crucified” by.
tossups.answer: the gold standard [accept answers which refer to the gold standard, or currency backed by gold, or the ability to redeem paper money/currency/bank notes as gold; prompt on Cross of Gold; prompt on specie payment or specie redemption or gold money or ending bimetallism; reject “gold coins”] (Eichengreen’s book is titled Gold Fetters; the bill in the second clue became the Specie Payment Resumption Act.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: the gold standard [accept answers which refer to the gold standard, or currency backed by gold, or the ability to redeem paper money/currency/bank notes as gold; prompt on Cross of Gold; prompt on specie payment or specie redemption or gold money or ending bimetallism; reject "gold coins"] (Eichengreen's book is titled Gold Fetters; the bill in the second clue became the Specie Payment Resumption Act.)

tossups.question: Philip Brett produced two editions of a pair of pieces by this composer for SSATB and ATTBB choir. A piece by this composer for six-part choir has alternating solo sections for nine soloists; that antiphon is Gaude gloriose Dei Mater. This composer wrote 17 pieces for a collection, including two Salvator Mundi and a seven-voice Miserere Nostri. An a cappella ensemble called this composer’s “Scholars” was founded by Peter Phillips. This composer began each verse with a Hebrew letter in five-voice settings of the first two lessons of the Tenebrae; those are the two Lamentations of Jeremiah. This composer collaborated with a student on the motet collection Cantiones sacrae. One of this composer’s contributions to Archbishop Parker’s Psalter is the Phrygian (“FRIDGE-ee-in”) melody “Why fum’th (“FYOOM-ith”) in fight.” For 10 points, name this English Renaissance composer of Spem in alium who inspired a Fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

For 10 points, name this English Renaissance composer of Spem in alium who inspired a Fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

tossups.question: This composer’s setting of Lamentations for five voices may support Richard Terry’s argument in Downside Review that this composer had lifelong allegiance to the Roman Catholic church. One work by this composer ends with the highest voice singing an F-sharp to G while the tenor sings a descending F-E-flat-D, a cross relationship that ends his work O nata lux de lumine. This composer set nine psalm tunes in memory of Archbishop Matthew Parker, including “Why Fum’th in Fight.” This composer wrote a motet for eighth different choirs and forty independent voice parts for the 40th birthday of Elizabeth I, who granted this composer and his pupil a monopoly on publishing polyphonic music. For 10 points, name this teacher of William Byrd, the English Renaissance composer of Spem in Alium.

tossups.question: The first entry in one piece by this composer in 4/2 [four-two] time begins with a dotted semibreve G, followed by three minim Gs, then a minim D. An English contrafactum for that piece used for the investiture of Henry Frederick as Prince of Wales begins ‘Sing and glorify heaven’s high Majesty’. The hymn “All praise to you, my God, this night” is based on this composer’s Canon. This composer’s Phrygian mode setting of Psalm 2 was used by another composer in a work for string orchestra. This composer contributed to a set of 34 motets, which was a financial failure despite holding a monopoly on printed music alongside his most famous pupil, William Byrd. For 10 points, name this English composer of Spem in alium who wrote a theme used in a Vaughan Williams Fantasia.

tossups.question: Only the Gloria, Sanctus, and Agnus Dei survive of this composer's seven-voice Christmas mass Puer natus est nobis. One of his works was commissioned by Thomas Howard to outdo Alessandro Striggio's (STREE-jo's) Ecce beatam lucem (ETCH-ay bay-AH-tahm LOO-chem). The Gimell Records label records the work of an early music ensemble founded by Peter Phillips called this musician's "Scholars." For the psalter of Archbishop Parker, this composer wrote the Phrygian (FRIDGE-ee-in) melody "Why fum'th (FUME-eth) in fight." He was eulogized with the piece Ye Sacred Muses by his student William Byrd, with whom he shared a monopoly on polyphonic music granted by Queen Elizabeth I. For 10 points, name this composer of the forty-voice motet Spem in alium whose music was also used for a namesake Fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

tossups.question: The first critical biography of this composer was published in 2016 and written by John Harley. In her book on this composer, Suzanne Cole traces the publication and reception history of his setting of the Preces, Responses, and Litany, which was deemed his greatest work during the 19th century. This composer’s unusually large-scale seven-voice motet Suscipe quaeso (soo-SKEE-pay KWY-soh) is sometimes thought to be related to his seven-voice mass Puer natus est nobis. Along with one of his students, this composer was granted a 21-year monopoly on printing music in 1575. In a piece by this composer, melodies are gradually passed between eight different choirs before a passage in which all forty voices sing together. For 10 points, name this teacher of William Byrd who composed the motet Spem in alium, as well as a theme used as the subject of a fantasia by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

tossups.question: Four instrumental In Nomines by this composer based on a Benedictus by one of his contemporaries are among only a handful of secular compositions by this composer. This composer employed one alto, two tenors, and two basses in the scoring for his liturgical setting of texts from the book of Lamentations. After this composer’s death, one contemporary produced the musical elegy Ye Sacred Muses. This composer followed the syllabic style of setting religious texts the anthem If ye love me and nine tunes for Archbishop Parker’s Psalter. This composer responded to the Duke of Norfolk’s challenge to produce a thirty part motet by writing a forty part one, Spem in Alium. For 10 points, name this English Renaissance composer.

tossups.question: WARNING: DESCRIPTION ACCEPTABLE The Supreme Court ruled against James Roosevelt, FDR’s father, in its first consideration of this policy, which upheld it. Charles Sumner’s opposition to this policy was one factor in his support for Horace Greeley in 1872. The author of Two Years Before the Mast, Richard Henry Dana Jr, launched a quixotic primary challenge to Congressman Benjamin Butler to oppose this policy, and Butler was the (*) 1884 Presidential nominee of the party founded to support it. Salmon Chase supported this policy as Treasury Secretary but opposed its constitutionality as Chief Justice, and FDR used the Trading With the Enemy Act of 1917 to implement this policy right after he was inaugurated. Richard Nixon put this policy in effect for good in 1973, the demise of the Bretton Woods system. For 10 points, what is this policy of printing money that is not backed by specie?
tossups.answer: greenbackism [accept word forms; accept “paper money,” “ending the Gold Standard,” and word forms, synonyms, and anything more elaborate. tossups.question: One party that advocated this cause elected Reinhold Sadler as governor of Nevada. Cuba-related amendment namesake Henry Moore Teller founded a political party named for this cause, which also included a senator from Idaho who was obsessed with disenfranchising Mormons, named Fred Dubois. Supporters of this cause attempted to force the passage of the Matthews resolution. One statement in favor of this cause stated that the controversy over it was “the issue of 1776 all over again”. Proponents of this cause advocated a (*) certain 16 to 1 ratio. This cause, which was heavily favored by indebted farmers and galvanized by the “Crime of ‘73”, was advanced by the Bland-Allison act and a law named for Sherman. For 10 points, name this cause advanced by William Jennings Bryan in the “Cross of Gold” speech, calling for the use of a certain metal in coinage.
tossups.answer: free silver [accept bimetallism, accept government buying silver or equivalents, any common-knowledge equivalents of the previous]

tossups.question: William McKinley appointed Adlai Ewing Stevenson to head a commission charged with international promotion of this cause. The Thomas Amendment to the AAA Act furthered this cause. This cause, promoted by William Harvey, was advanced by an 1878 piece of legislation that authorized $2.5 million to be used for this cause, whose proponents, many of which were farmers, were angered by what they called “the (*) Crime of ‘73." An 1890 piece of legislation advancing this cause established a 16.5 to 1 ratio of exchange but was repealed when it almost resulted in bankruptcy. Advanced by the Bland-Allison and Sherman Acts, for 10 points, identify this cause championed by William Jennings Bryan to allow two types of specie to be used for currency.
tossups.answer: bimetallism or free silver [accept anything that implies silver could be used for coinage as well as gold]

parts: Name this setting of Psalm 2, the third of nine tunes written for Archbishop Matthew Parker. The tenor line in this tune begins with the notes [read slowly] E, G, G, long G, G, A, A, long B.

“Why Fum’th in Sight” was written by this English composer. The hymn tune Glory to thee, my God, this night is taken from another work in Archbishop Parker’s Psalter and is known as this composer’s namesake Canon.
“Why Fum’th in Sight” formed the basis of this composer’s piece for double string orchestra Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis. This composer also wrote The Lark Ascending.
parts_sanitized: Name this setting of Psalm 2, the third of nine tunes written for Archbishop Matthew Parker. The tenor line in this tune begins with the notes [read slowly] E, G, G, long G, G, A, A, long B.

"Why Fum'th in Sight" was written by this English composer. The hymn tune Glory to thee, my God, this night is taken from another work in Archbishop Parker's Psalter and is known as this composer's namesake Canon.
"Why Fum'th in Sight" formed the basis of this composer's piece for double string orchestra Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis. This composer also wrote The Lark Ascending.
answers: “Why Fum’th(“f’yoomth”) in Sight” [accept “Why Fum’th in Fight”] (This is a common misspelling probably originating from the spelling ſight, with a long s. The original Hebrew has no word corresponding to “sight” or “fight”.)
Thomas Tallis [or Thomas Talles; or Thomas Tallys; accept Tallis’ Canon]

Thomas Tallis [or Thomas Talles; or Thomas Tallys; accept Tallis' Canon]

bonuses.leadin: Despite being Catholic and publishing Mass Propers in his Gradualia, this composer managed to prosper under a Protestant regime for decades. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Catholic English composer, who co-composed a set of Cantiones Sacrae along with an older composer who may have been his teacher. Along with that older composer, he held a monopoly on printing music for 21 years.

Like much Renaissance music, Byrd’s motet Aspice Domine de sede (“AH-spee-che do-mee-ne”) is a polyphonic piece based around one of these pre-existing melodies, known by a Latin term. These melodies were usually placed in the tenor voice.

This English queen granted Byrd and Tallis the monopoly on music printing, and was the dedicatee of their Cantiones Sacrae. Although she was a Protestant, she kept Byrd around in order to raise her court’s reputation.
answers: William Byrd (The older composer is Thomas Tallis.)

Thomas Tallis composed settings for the "Lamentations" of this Old Testament Prophet to be sung at the Tenebrae Service on Maundy Thursday.

bonuses.leadin: In 1575, Queen Elizabeth gave this man and William Byrd a monopoly for the printing of music and music paper in England. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this English composer of the five part Salve Intemerata mass as well as the Miserere Nostri. He also composed the forty-part motet Spem in Alium.
This man composed Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis. A musical editor of The English Hymnal, this man also composed Fantasia on Greensleeves.
This Vaughan Williams piece was prefaced by its namesake George Meredith poem, which states, “He rises and begins to round/He drops the silver chain of sound.”
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In 1575, Queen Elizabeth gave this man and William Byrd a monopoly for the printing of music and music paper in England. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this English composer of the five part Salve Intemerata mass as well as the Miserere Nostri. He also composed the forty-part motet Spem in Alium.
This man composed Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis. A musical editor of The English Hymnal, this man also composed Fantasia on Greensleeves.
This Vaughan Williams piece was prefaced by its namesake George Meredith poem, which states, "He rises and begins to round/He drops the silver chain of sound."

bonuses.leadin: This work was commissioned after the performance of a similar piece by Alessandro Striggio, and it may have been written for the queen of England’s fortieth birthday. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this piece of music, a motet scored for eight choirs of fives voices each, that takes its text from a passage in the Book of Judith.
Spem in Alium was composed by this contemporary of William Byrd who wrote a ton of religious music. A notable “Fantasia on a Theme by” him was composed by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

This contemporary artist’s Forty Part Motet consists of playing the forty parts of Spem in Alium from different locations and allowing people to walk through the exhibit. Her works with George Bures Miller include The Murder of Crows and The Killing Machine.
answers_sanitized: Spem in Alium [or Hope in Any Other]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This work was commissioned after the performance of a similar piece by Alessandro Striggio, and it may have been written for the queen of England's fortieth birthday. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this piece of music, a motet scored for eight choirs of fives voices each, that takes its text from a passage in the Book of Judith.
Spem in Alium was composed by this contemporary of William Byrd who wrote a ton of religious music. A notable "Fantasia on a Theme by" him was composed by Ralph Vaughan Williams.

This contemporary artist's Forty Part Motet consists of playing the forty parts of Spem in Alium from different locations and allowing people to walk through the exhibit. Her works with George Bures Miller include The Murder of Crows and The Killing Machine.

bonuses.leadin: The theme for this piece is in the Phrygian mode and was originally part of a hymnal for Archbishop Matthew Parker. For 10 points each:
parts: A certain composer’s setting of “Why Fum’th in Fight” provides the basis of what piece that splits a string orchestra into three parts of differing size?
Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis is by this English composer of Fantasia on Greensleves and The Lark Ascending.
This contemporary of Thomas Tallis collaborated with him on a series of motets called Cantiones Sacrae. His keyboard output includes My Ladye Nevells Booke.
answers: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis [or Tallis Fantasia]
answers_sanitized: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis [or Tallis Fantasia]
